title: Authority Hierarchies: Federal vs State Jurisdiction
content:
Federal OSHA Authority:

Federal OSHA operates under the General Duty Clause of 29 CFR 1910. They do not have specific laser interlock requirements but enforce industry best practices. When violations are found, they reference ANSI Z136.1-2014 and may refer to FDA codes under 21 CFR 1040.

State OSHA Authority:

State OSHA programs may or may not specifically address Laser Interlocks - it depends on the individual state. Some states have adopted specific laser safety requirements while others follow federal OSHA guidelines.

FDA CDRH Authority:

The Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH) has federal mandate over laser product manufacturing standards. Their authority covers:
- Performance standards for laser products (21 CFR 1040.10)
- Specific use laser products (21 CFR 1040.11)
- Import/export requirements
- Records and reporting requirements

Authority Overlap:

- FDA regulates MANUFACTURERS of laser products
- OSHA regulates EMPLOYERS using laser products in the workplace
- ANSI provides the standards that both reference
plain_language_summary: Think of it this way: FDA makes sure laser PRODUCTS are built safely. OSHA makes sure laser USERS work safely. States can add their own rules on top.

Federal_OSHA.authority: Workplace safety for most private employers
Federal_OSHA.laser_specific: No specific laser regulations - enforces via 'best practices'
Federal_OSHA.covers: Most private sector employers in 29 states

State_OSHA.laser_specific: Varies by state - some have specific laser requirements
State_OSHA.examples: Some states require stricter interlock usage than federal

FDA_CDRH.laser_specific: Detailed performance standards for all laser products
FDA_CDRH.enforcement: Products cannot be legally sold without compliance

ANSI.importance: Provides specific guidance that OSHA uses for enforcement

state_vs_federal.key_point: State OSHA programs can be MORE strict than federal, but not less
state_vs_federal.what_this_means: If you operate in multiple states, you must know each state's requirements

examples.stricter_states: Some states have specific laser interlock requirements
examples.federal_alike: Many states follow federal OSHA guidelines exactly

practical_implications.manufacturers: Must comply with FDA + OSHA workplace safety for employees
practical_implications.employers: Must follow OSHA (state or federal) + ANSI best practices
practical_implications.users: Should verify product FDA compliance + follow employer OSHA program
